claimed_contribution: The paper shows that base-rate effects in product-harm crisis attribution are conditional, not uniform: effects depend on prior brand beliefs and on whether similarity information makes the base rate case-relevant.

It identifies two distinct attribution-adjustment patterns from base-rate information: discounting (high industry frequency with similarity information) and subtyping (low industry frequency without similarity information).
It demonstrates that these two processes have different carryover effects to later crises: discounting tends to recur for a second crisis, whereas subtyping of a first crisis is unlikely to recur.
context: The study is set in consumer attributions of product-harm crises, such as product recalls and illness incidents linked to brands. The empirical context is beer-brand crisis news scenarios, using both fictitious and real brands.
design_and_data: Experiment 1 used a 2 x 2 x 2 between-subjects design (prior beliefs: positive vs negative; industry frequency: high vs low; similarity information: present vs absent) with 192 student participants and a fictitious beer brand (Stiegal). Participants read brand-background materials and then a crisis article; outcomes included blame assignment, brand trustworthiness, brand evaluation, and a subtyping index, with manipulation checks for industry frequency and similarity. A follow-up experiment (n = 106) used weaker base-rate levels and thought listings to assess spontaneous attributional processing; an additional experiment with existing brands (n = 257; Heineken vs Lakeport) replicated the core interaction pattern on trustworthiness and evaluation. Experiment 2 used a two-part design: first-crisis phase (n = 134; 2 x 2 plus control) and, three days later, a second-crisis phase for participants from the discounting and subtyping conditions plus a new control group (n = 25), measuring blame, brand evaluation, and locus of cause.
main_findings: For brands with positive prior beliefs, high (vs low) industry frequency reduced blame and improved brand trust/evaluation when similarity information was present, but when similarity information was absent, low (vs high) industry frequency reduced blame and improved evaluations.
For brands with negative prior beliefs, blame, trustworthiness, and brand evaluations were not significantly affected by industry frequency or similarity information.

In sequential crises, participants who discounted the first crisis also discounted the second under the same high-frequency/similarity-present condition, whereas participants who subtyped the first crisis did not subtype the second under the same low-frequency/similarity-absent condition; locus-of-cause shifts away from the brand occurred in discounting but not subtyping.
one_line_summary: The paper finds that base-rate information shapes product-harm crisis blame only under specific conditions, producing discounting or subtyping for positively viewed brands and showing that only discounting carries forward to a subsequent crisis.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior base-rate attribution research by specifying boundary conditions in product-harm crises, namely prior brand beliefs and case-specific similarity information. It also adds a second effect pattern, subtyping, alongside the conventional discounting account and shows different downstream effects across repeated crises.
research_gap: Prior studies report inconsistent effects of base-rate information on attributions, so it is unclear whether and when industry frequency information influences blame in product-harm crises. The paper addresses this by testing boundary conditions that were not clearly integrated in earlier work, especially prior brand beliefs and the case-specific relevance of base-rate information via similarity.
research_question: Does base-rate information about how common product-harm crises are in an industry influence consumers' attribution of blame to a focal brand? Under what conditions do consumers discount blame versus subtype a crisis as an exception, and how do these processes affect attribution of a subsequent crisis for the same brand?
